title: Claude de Berlaymont

(or **Claudius van Barlaymont**), lord of Haultpenne (ca. 1550 – 14 July 1587), was a Flemish military commander in [Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain")'s [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders") during the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War").
[Jean Charles Joseph de Vegiano](/wiki/Jean_Charles_Joseph_de_Vegiano "Jean Charles Joseph de Vegiano"), *Nobiliaire des Pays-Bas et du comté de 1724-94 Bourgogne,*1 – 1865, p 162.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

| Claude de Berlaymont | |
| --- | --- |

| Nickname | [Haultpenne](/wiki/Haultpenne "Haultpenne") |
| Born | circa 1550 |
| Died | 14 July 1587 (aged 36-37)  ['s-Hertogenbosch](/wiki/%27s-Hertogenbosch "'s-Hertogenbosch") |
| Service years | 1579–1587 |
| Conflicts | [Siege of Breda (1581)](/wiki/Siege_of_Breda_(1581) "Siege of Breda (1581)") [Battle of Werl (1586)](/wiki/Battle_of_Werl_(1586) "Battle of Werl (1586)") |

**Claude de Berlaymont** (or **Claudius van Barlaymont**), lord of Haultpenne (ca. 1550 – 14 July 1587), was a Flemish military commander in [Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain")'s [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders") during the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War").

## Family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Claude_de_Berlaymont&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Family")]

He was the seventh and last son of count [Charles de Berlaymont](/wiki/Charles_de_Berlaymont "Charles de Berlaymont") (1510–1578) and Adriana de Ligne Barbançon.[[1]](#cite_note-1) His father was a supporter and councilor to [Margaret of Parma](/wiki/Margaret_of_Parma "Margaret of Parma"); he is reported to have referred to the Dutch citizens requesting the [Compromise of Nobles](/wiki/Compromise_of_Nobles "Compromise of Nobles") as "[geuzen](/wiki/Geuzen "Geuzen")" (beggars), an appellation the Dutch proudly adopted.

### Siblings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Claude_de_Berlaymont&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Siblings")]

* [Gilles of Berlaymont](/wiki/Gilles_van_Berlaymont "Gilles van Berlaymont"), count of [Hierges](/wiki/Hierges "Hierges"), stadtholder of [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland") (1572–1574), [Guelders](/wiki/Guelders "Guelders") (1572–1577), [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(province) "Groningen (province)") (1572–1574). He died in the [Siege of Maastricht (1579)](/wiki/Siege_of_Maastricht_(1579) "Siege of Maastricht (1579)").
* John of Berlaymont, provost of the Saint Gervais, in [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht"), and a member of the cathedral chapter in [Liege](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège").
* Charles of Berlaymont, [Knight of Malta](/wiki/Knights_Hospitaller "Knights Hospitaller") in 1554
* Louis of Berlaymont (? – 15 February 1596), archbishop of Cambrai
* [Lancelot of Berlaymont](/wiki/Lancelot_of_Berlaymont "Lancelot of Berlaymont"), lord of Beauraing
* [Florent of Berlaymont](/wiki/Florent_of_Berlaymont "Florent of Berlaymont") (? – 1626), count of Berlaymont, lord of Floyon, knight of the Golden Fleece, hereditary chamberlain of Hainault, and governor of Luxembourg.[[2]](#cite_note-2)
* Marie of Berlaymont
* Adrienne of Berlaymont (? – 1582), married Johann of Brandenburg

He married Adrienne de Brimeu, but left no offspring. At his death, his property and honors passed to his brother, Florent.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Claude_de_Berlaymont&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Career")]

Claude was lord of Haultpenne in [Flémalle](/wiki/Fl%C3%A9malle "Flémalle"), a Walloon area in the bishopric of Liege. His nickname is derived from the name of the castle, an elegant medieval and Renaissance structure. He was a member of the council of states of the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces"), and he was captured in 1576 with his father. Although he signed the [Union of Brussels](/wiki/Union_of_Brussels "Union of Brussels"), but immediately afterwards travelled to Namur, where he joined the [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") faction and tried to win the favour of [Don John of Austria](/wiki/Don_John_of_Austria "Don John of Austria"). His father died in 1578, and in 1579, his oldest brother, Gilles, killed at the [Siege of Maastricht](/wiki/Siege_of_Maastricht_(1579) "Siege of Maastricht (1579)"); Claude succeeded his brother as governor of [Charleville](/wiki/Charleville-M%C3%A9zi%C3%A8res "Charleville-Mézières").

In 1581 Haultpenne was sent to fight Dutch rebels in the eastern province of [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"), but the capture of [Steenbergen](/wiki/Steenbergen "Steenbergen") on 12 August 1583 signalled the need for his return to [Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"). In 1581 he led the [Siege of Breda](/wiki/Siege_of_Breda_(1581) "Siege of Breda (1581)"), also known as the *Haultepenne's Fury*. Breda had been in the hands of [William of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") since the [Pacification of Ghent](/wiki/Pacification_of_Ghent "Pacification of Ghent") in 1576. Haultpenne managed to enter the city by bribing a sentry, after which his troops sacked and plundered it, murdering more than 500 citizens. He defended ['s-Hertogenbosch](/wiki/%27s-Hertogenbosch "'s-Hertogenbosch"), and reconquered [Lier](/wiki/Lier,_Belgium "Lier, Belgium"), [Eindhoven](/wiki/Eindhoven "Eindhoven"), [Nijmegen](/wiki/Nijmegen "Nijmegen") and [Guelders](/wiki/Guelders "Guelders"), after which he became its [Stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") from 1583–1585.

The [Duke of Parma](/wiki/Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Alexander Farnese, Duke of Parma") had great confidence in Haultpenne's abilities, and sent him to the [Electorate of Cologne](/wiki/Electorate_of_Cologne "Electorate of Cologne"), which was rife with disorder arising out of conflicting claims by Calvinists and Catholics to the Electorate and its possessions. In the [Cologne War](/wiki/Cologne_War "Cologne War"), Berlaymount laid siege to [Werl](/wiki/Battle_of_Werl_(1586) "Battle of Werl (1586)") in 1586,. Although [Martin Schenck von Nydeggen](/wiki/Martin_Schenck_von_Nydeggen "Martin Schenck von Nydeggen") had a force of approximately 500 men, and Berlaymount over 4000 plus horse and cannons, Schenck succeeded in fighting his way out of the city and to safety. While at [Boxtel](/wiki/Boxtel "Boxtel"), Haultpenne received a call for aid from 's- Hertogenbosch. On his way there, he engaged an enemy army led by [Philip of Hohenlohe](/wiki/Philip_of_Hohenlohe "Philip of Hohenlohe") at a [redoubt](/wiki/Redoubt "Redoubt") at [Engelen](/wiki/Engelen "Engelen"), and was mortally wounded in the throat. He was taken to 's-Hertogenbosch, where he died on 14 July.
